Doctor: Good afternoon, Mr. Daniels. I’m Dr. Patel, the cardiologist. I’ve reviewed your chart, but I want to hear from you directly. What brought you into the hospital this time?

Patient: I’ve just been so short of breath. It’s been getting worse for a while, but the last week or so it really got bad. My legs are huge, and I can’t lie flat without feeling like I’m drowning.

Doctor: When did you first notice your breathing getting worse?

Patient: Maybe a month ago I started getting more winded walking to the mailbox. I used to be able to walk around the block with my wife. Then in the last week, I can barely make it across the living room. Two nights ago I woke up gasping for air.

Doctor: How many pillows do you sleep on now?

Patient: Four. I used to sleep with one. Now if I lie flat, I start coughing and can’t catch my breath.

Doctor: How often are you waking up at night short of breath?

Patient: Almost every night this past week. I have to sit on the edge of the bed and wait for it to calm down.

Doctor: Tell me about the leg swelling.

Patient: My ankles started swelling a couple of months ago, but I thought it was the heat. Now my legs are swollen up to my knees. My shoes don’t fit. My skin feels tight and shiny.

Doctor: Any weight changes?

Patient: I’ve gained about 15 pounds in the last month, but I’m not eating more. My wife says it’s all fluid.

Doctor: Any chest pain or pressure?

Patient: Not really pain. Sometimes I feel a heaviness in my chest when I walk, but it goes away when I sit down. No sharp pain or anything going down my arm.

Doctor: Any palpitations or feeling like your heart is racing or skipping beats?

Patient: Yeah, my heart feels like it’s pounding sometimes, especially at night when I’m lying there trying to breathe.

Doctor: Any dizziness or fainting?

Patient: I get lightheaded when I stand up too fast, but I haven’t passed out.

Doctor: Any cough?

Patient: Yes, a dry cough mostly. Sometimes a little frothy spit, but no blood.

Doctor: Any recent fevers, chills, or infections?

Patient: No, I haven’t been sick otherwise.

Doctor: Let’s review your heart history. When were you first diagnosed with heart failure?

Patient: About three years ago. I had a heart attack and they said my heart was weak. They put in two stents.

Doctor: Do you know your last ejection fraction, the pumping function of your heart?

Patient: Last time they told me it was around 25 percent.

Doctor: Have you been hospitalized for heart failure before?

Patient: Yes, twice. Once when they first diagnosed me, and once last year when I got really swollen and short of breath. They gave me IV water pills and I lost a lot of fluid.

Doctor: What heart medications are you taking at home?

Patient: I take carvedilol 25 milligrams twice a day, sacubitril/valsartan—the Entresto—49/51 twice a day, spironolactone 25 once a day, furosemide 40 milligrams twice a day, and empagliflozin 10 milligrams once a day. I also take aspirin 81, atorvastatin 80 at night, and clopidogrel because of the stents.

Doctor: Have there been any changes to your medications recently?

Patient: My primary doctor increased my furosemide from once a day to twice a day about a month ago when my legs started swelling more. Otherwise, it’s been the same.

Doctor: How often do you miss doses?

Patient: I’m pretty good with my morning pills. Sometimes I miss the evening ones if I fall asleep in the recliner. Maybe a couple of times a week.

Doctor: Do you follow a low-salt diet?

Patient: I try, but I’m not perfect. I like canned soups and deli meats, and I know those are salty. My wife nags me about it.

Doctor: Any other medical conditions?

Patient: I have type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and chronic kidney disease. They said my kidneys are stage 3. I also have sleep apnea and use a CPAP, but I don’t use it every night.

Doctor: What do you take for your diabetes?

Patient: Metformin 1000 twice a day, insulin glargine 32 units at night, and the empagliflozin you mentioned. My sugars run around 150–200 most days.

Doctor: Any allergies to medications?

Patient: I had a bad cough with lisinopril. No other drug allergies.

Doctor: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?

Patient: I quit smoking 10 years ago. I have a beer or two on the weekends.

Doctor: Any illicit drug use?

Patient: No.

Doctor: How has this been affecting your daily life?

Patient: I can’t do much. I used to help my wife with groceries and yard work. Now I get winded just walking to the bathroom. I’ve been sleeping in the recliner because I can’t lie flat. I’m scared to go up the stairs.

Doctor: I’m going to summarize what’s been done since you arrived. In the emergency department, your blood pressure was 158 over 92, heart rate 112, respiratory rate 24, oxygen saturation 88% on room air, and you were breathing fast and using extra muscles. They put you on 2 liters of oxygen and your saturation improved to 94%. Your weight on admission was 104 kg; your clinic weight a month ago was 97 kg.

Patient: Yeah, I knew I was up, but I didn’t think it was that much.

Doctor: On exam today, you have elevated neck veins at 45 degrees, your lungs have crackles halfway up on both sides, and your heart is fast but regular. Your abdomen is mildly distended with some fluid wave, and you have 3+ pitting edema up to your knees. No focal neurologic deficits.

Patient: That sounds about how I feel.

Doctor: Your labs show a BNP of 2,400, which is quite elevated, indicating significant heart strain. Your creatinine is 2.0, up from your baseline of 1.5, so your kidneys are worse. Your potassium is 4.8, sodium 132, and your liver enzymes are mildly elevated. Your troponin is 0.03, at the upper limit of normal, and your EKG shows sinus tachycardia with left bundle branch block, which you’ve had before. Your chest X-ray shows an enlarged heart and fluid in your lungs and a small right pleural effusion.

Patient: So it’s my heart again?

Doctor: Yes. You’re in acute decompensated heart failure with volume overload—what we call “wet and warm” heart failure. You’re carrying a lot of extra fluid in your lungs, legs, and abdomen, and your kidneys are struggling because of it.

Patient: Am I going to need that breathing machine?

Doctor: Right now, you’re maintaining your oxygen on nasal cannula, and you’re not in severe respiratory distress. If your breathing worsens, we might use BiPAP to help, but at this point we can continue with oxygen and aggressive diuresis.

Patient: What are you going to do to get this fluid off?

Doctor: We’ve already started IV furosemide 80 mg twice a day, and you’ve put out about 1.5 liters of urine since last night. We’ll likely increase the dose and/or add a second diuretic like metolazone if needed. We’ll monitor your weight, urine output, kidney function, and electrolytes closely.

Patient: Will you change my other heart meds?

Doctor: For now:
- We’ll continue your carvedilol at the current dose if your blood pressure and heart rate tolerate it.
- We’ll hold your sacubitril/valsartan temporarily because of your rising creatinine and low-ish blood pressure, and reassess once you’re more euvolemic.
- We’ll continue spironolactone and empagliflozin if your kidney function and potassium allow, but we may need to adjust.
- We’ll continue aspirin and atorvastatin; we’ll discuss with cardiology whether to continue clopidogrel long-term given how long it’s been since your stents.

Patient: Am I going to need more procedures?

Doctor: We’ve ordered an echocardiogram to reassess your heart function and valve status. Depending on the results and your symptoms, we may consider:
- Optimizing your guideline-directed medical therapy.
- Evaluating you for device therapy if not already done—such as an ICD (defibrillator) or CRT (biventricular pacemaker) given your low EF and left bundle branch block.
- If your symptoms remain severe despite optimal therapy, we may discuss advanced heart failure options with the HF team.

Patient: I don’t want to end up on a transplant list or something.

Doctor: We’re not there yet, and many patients improve significantly with optimized medications and careful fluid management. But it’s important to plan ahead and know what options exist. We’ll also talk about your goals and what matters most to you.

Patient: I just want to be able to walk with my wife again.

Doctor: That’s a good goal. In the short term, we’ll focus on:
- Getting the extra fluid off.
- Improving your breathing and sleep.
- Adjusting your medications safely.
Once you’re more stable, we’ll:
- Refer you to cardiac rehab.
- Reinforce a low-sodium diet and fluid restriction.
- Address your CPAP use and make sure your sleep apnea is treated, which will help your heart.

Patient: How long will I be in the hospital?

Doctor: Likely several days—long enough to get your fluid status under control, stabilize your kidneys and electrolytes, and make sure your medications are at safe doses. We’ll also involve our heart failure nurse and possibly a case manager to help with education and follow-up planning.

Patient: Okay. I’m tired of feeling like this.

Doctor: I understand. We’ll work with you and your wife to get you feeling better and to reduce the chance of coming back to the hospital. I’ll check on you again later today after we see how you respond to the diuretics and after we have your echocardiogram results.

Patient: Thank you, doctor.

Doctor: Good afternoon, Mr. Harris. I’m Dr. Coleman, the gastroenterologist. I’ve reviewed some of your chart, but I’d like to hear from you directly. What’s been going on with your stomach and bowels?

Patient: For the last few months, I’ve been having a lot of diarrhea and stomach cramps. It’s gotten worse in the past few weeks.

Doctor: When you say “a lot of diarrhea,” how many bowel movements are you having in a day?

Patient: On a bad day, 8 to 10 times. On a good day, maybe 4 or 5. It used to be once or twice a day before this started.

Doctor: What do the stools look like? Watery, loose, formed? Any blood or mucus?

Patient: Mostly loose and watery. Sometimes there’s mucus. I’ve seen blood on and off—sometimes it’s mixed in, sometimes it’s on the paper or in the toilet.

Doctor: How long have you been seeing blood?

Patient: Maybe three months? At first it was just a little streak now and then, but lately it’s more often. Not every time, but a few times a week.

Doctor: Any black, tarry stools?

Patient: No, more red than black.

Doctor: Tell me more about the cramps. Where is the pain, and what does it feel like?

Patient: It’s mostly low down in my belly, on the left side and across the bottom. It feels crampy, like someone twisting my insides. It gets worse before I have to go to the bathroom and eases up a bit after.

Doctor: On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is the pain at its worst?

Patient: Maybe a 7. It’s not constant at that level, but it comes in waves.

Doctor: Any nausea or vomiting?

Patient: I feel queasy sometimes, but I haven’t really thrown up.

Doctor: Any fevers, chills, or night sweats?

Patient: I’ve had some low-grade fevers at home—99, 100. I wake up sweaty some nights, but not drenched.

Doctor: How’s your appetite?

Patient: Not great. I don’t feel like eating much because I’m afraid it’ll send me running to the bathroom.

Doctor: Have you lost any weight?

Patient: Yeah, about 15 pounds in the last 3 or 4 months without trying.

Doctor: Any problems swallowing, heartburn, or upper belly pain?

Patient: No, not really. It’s mostly lower down.

Doctor: Any joint pains, eye redness, or skin rashes?

Patient: My knees and ankles have been achy, especially in the mornings. I thought it was just getting older. No eye redness. I did have a rash on my shins a few weeks ago—red spots that were a little raised and tender. It went away after about a week.

Doctor: Any mouth sores?

Patient: I’ve had a couple of canker sores, but I get those sometimes anyway.

Doctor: Any recent travel, camping, or changes in your diet?

Patient: No travel. I haven’t changed my diet much, though I’ve been eating less. No well water or anything like that.

Doctor: Have you taken any antibiotics in the last few months?

Patient: I took a Z-pack for a sinus infection about two months ago.

Doctor: Any history of similar bowel problems in the past?

Patient: No, this is new. I’ve had hemorrhoids before, but never this kind of diarrhea and pain.

Doctor: Let’s go over your medical history. What other conditions do you have?

Patient: I have type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and I had a heart attack 4 years ago. They put in two stents. I also have psoriasis on my elbows and scalp. My primary doctor said my liver tests were a little high last year, maybe fatty liver.

Doctor: What medications are you taking?

Patient: Metformin 1000 twice a day, insulin glargine 24 units at night, lisinopril 20 milligrams once a day, metoprolol 50 milligrams twice a day, atorvastatin 80 at night, aspirin 81, and clopidogrel 75 because of the stents. For the psoriasis, I use a steroid cream and I started adalimumab injections about 6 months ago. I also take omeprazole for heartburn.

Doctor: Any allergies to medications?

Patient: Penicillin gives me a rash. No other drug allergies.

Doctor: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?

Patient: I quit smoking after my heart attack. I used to smoke a pack a day. I drink a beer or two on the weekends.

Doctor: Any family history of bowel disease or colon cancer?

Patient: My mom had ulcerative colitis. She ended up having surgery to remove her colon in her 50s. My dad had colon polyps but no cancer.

Doctor: Have you ever had a colonoscopy?

Patient: Yes, about 5 years ago. They found a couple of small polyps and removed them. They told me to come back in 5 years, but I haven’t yet.

Doctor: Since you came to the hospital, we’ve done some tests. When you arrived, your blood pressure was 128 over 76, heart rate 104, temperature 100.4°F, and oxygen level 99% on room air. On exam, your abdomen was soft but tender in the left lower quadrant and across the lower abdomen, with some mild guarding but no rebound. Bowel sounds were present but a bit hyperactive. No obvious external hemorrhoids or fissures on rectal exam, but there was some bright red blood on the glove.

Patient: Yeah, that exam wasn’t fun.

Doctor: Your labs show a white blood cell count of 12,500, hemoglobin 10.2 (down from 13.1 a year ago), platelets 420,000. Your CRP is elevated at 48, and ESR is 62. Your creatinine is 1.1, which is your baseline. Your liver tests show mildly elevated ALT and AST, similar to before. Your stool tests are positive for fecal calprotectin and lactoferrin, which indicate inflammation in the intestines. Stool cultures and C. difficile toxin are negative so far.

Patient: So it’s not an infection?

Doctor: The tests so far don’t show a typical bacterial infection or C. diff. Given your symptoms, family history, and the inflammatory markers, we’re concerned about inflammatory bowel disease—most likely ulcerative colitis or possibly Crohn’s disease involving the colon.

Patient: Like what my mom had?

Doctor: Yes, ulcerative colitis runs in families. To make a definite diagnosis and see how extensive it is, we need to look directly at your colon.

Patient: A colonoscopy?

Doctor: Because you’re having active symptoms and bleeding, we’ll do a flexible sigmoidoscopy first—looking at the rectum and left side of the colon—with biopsies. Depending on what we see and how stable you are, we may do a full colonoscopy later. The images and biopsies will help us distinguish between ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s, and other causes.

Patient: Is it safe to do that while I’m bleeding?

Doctor: Yes. You’re not bleeding heavily, and your vital signs are stable. We’ll give you a limited prep so we can see the lower colon clearly. The procedure has some risks, like bleeding or perforation, but those are low, and the information we get is very important for guiding treatment.

Patient: What about the adalimumab I’m taking for psoriasis? Could that be causing this?

Doctor: Adalimumab is actually a medication we use to treat inflammatory bowel disease, so it’s unlikely to be causing it. In some cases, people on certain biologics can develop other autoimmune issues, but in your case it may be that the dose or frequency isn’t enough to control both the skin and the gut, or that the bowel disease developed despite it. We’ll coordinate with your dermatologist and possibly adjust your regimen.

Patient: What happens if it is ulcerative colitis?

Doctor: Treatment depends on severity and extent. For moderate to severe disease like yours, we often use:
- Steroids to quickly reduce inflammation.
- Maintenance therapy with medications like mesalamine, immunomodulators, or biologics (including anti-TNF drugs like adalimumab, or others).
We’ll also address your anemia and nutrition and monitor for complications.

Patient: Will I need surgery like my mom?

Doctor: Many patients are managed medically for years without surgery. Surgery is considered if medications don’t control the disease, if there are severe complications, or if there’s a high risk of cancer. It’s too early to say in your case; our focus now is diagnosis and initial control of inflammation.

Patient: Okay. I’m worried about the blood thinners with all this bleeding.

Doctor: That’s an important point. You’re on aspirin and clopidogrel because of your stents. We’ll involve cardiology to weigh the risks and benefits. For now, given your active GI bleeding, we may hold aspirin temporarily and continue clopidogrel, but that decision will be made carefully with cardiology input.

Patient: What’s the plan for today and the next few days?

Doctor: Today:
- Keep you on IV fluids and a clear liquid diet.
- Start IV steroids after we get the endoscopy, if the findings are consistent with inflammatory bowel disease.
- Continue your PPI (omeprazole) and hold NSAIDs (which you’re already avoiding).
- Monitor your blood counts and inflammatory markers.
- Coordinate with anesthesia and endoscopy for the flexible sigmoidoscopy and biopsies.

Over the next few days:
- Review biopsy results.
- Start or adjust long-term IBD therapy.
- Treat your anemia with iron, possibly IV if needed.
- Optimize your diabetes and cardiovascular medications around the new diagnosis.

Patient: Alright. I just want the diarrhea and cramps to stop.

Doctor: That’s our goal. We’ll proceed step by step, keep you informed, and involve you in decisions about long-term treatment.

Doctor: Any fevers or chills?

Doctor: Any chest pain?

Doctor: I’m going to review what’s been done since you arrived. Your EKG shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response around 120, and nonspecific ST-T changes, no ST elevation.

Doctor: It tells us you have:
- A COPD exacerbation with CO2 retention.
- Worsening heart failure with fluid in your lungs and legs.
- Likely an infection—pneumonia—given the fever, elevated white count, and chest X-ray.
- Your heart is under strain, which explains the mild troponin rise, and your kidneys are also stressed.

Patient: Am I having another heart attack?

Doctor: Your troponin is slightly elevated, but your EKG doesn’t show a classic heart attack pattern, and your chest discomfort is more pressure with exertion that improves with rest. This is most likely “demand ischemia”—your heart muscle is stressed by the infection, low oxygen, fast heart rate, and fluid overload. We’ll trend your troponins and repeat EKGs to be sure.

Patient: Am I going to need that BiPAP mask again?

Doctor: Very possibly. Right now you’re maintaining your oxygen on nasal cannula, but your CO2 is elevated and you’re working hard to breathe. If your breathing worsens or your CO2 goes up further, we’ll start BiPAP to support your breathing and try to avoid a breathing tube.

Patient: I really don’t want to be put on a ventilator.

Doctor: I understand. Our goal is to avoid intubation if at all possible. We’ve already started:
- IV steroids.
- Frequent nebulized bronchodilators.
- IV diuretics to remove excess fluid.
- IV antibiotics to treat pneumonia.
- Medications to slow your heart rate.
We’re admitting you to a step-down or ICU level of care for close monitoring.

Patient: What about my kidneys?

Doctor: Your creatinine is higher than usual, likely from the combination of heart failure, diuretics, infection, and low blood pressure at times. We’ll:
- Adjust your diuretic and heart failure medications carefully.
- Temporarily hold or reduce medications that can worsen kidney function if needed, like your ACE/ARB-equivalent (Entresto) depending on your pressures and labs.
- Monitor your kidney function and electrolytes daily.

Patient: Am I going to die from this?

Doctor: You’re very sick, and this is a serious flare of both your lungs and heart, but you’re in the right place and we’re treating all the contributing problems. Many people with COPD and heart failure have severe exacerbations and recover with aggressive treatment. We’ll also talk about your wishes if things were to get worse.

Patient: I don’t want to be kept alive on machines forever, but I do want you to try to help me.

Doctor: That’s very helpful. For now, we’ll list you as full code, meaning we would do CPR and intubation if absolutely necessary, but we can revisit this as we see how you respond and after you talk with your family. We can also involve palliative care to help with symptom control and planning.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: In the hospital, we’ll:
- Continue oxygen, bronchodilators, steroids, diuretics, antibiotics, and rate control for your atrial fibrillation.
- Monitor your heart rhythm, oxygen levels, blood gases, and kidney function closely.
- Adjust your heart failure and diabetes medications as needed.
Once you’re more stable, we’ll:
- Reassess your need for home oxygen.
- Review your inhaler technique and heart failure self-care.
- Discuss pulmonary and cardiac rehab and what to watch for at home.

Patient: I just want to be able to breathe and not be back here every few months.

Doctor: That’s our goal. We’ll work with you and your wife to get you through this flare and reduce your risk of future hospitalizations.

Doctor: Hi, Ms. Johnson. I’m Dr. Patel, the rheumatologist. I know you were referred here because of joint pain and some abnormal blood tests. I’d like to hear your story in your own words. What’s been going on?

Patient: My joints have been hurting for about a year, but it’s gotten a lot worse in the last few months. My hands, wrists, and knees are the worst. They’re stiff in the mornings, and I feel exhausted all the time.

Doctor: Let’s break that down. When did you first notice joint problems?

Patient: Maybe last spring. My fingers would ache after a long day at work, but I thought it was just getting older. Then over the summer it started happening more often, and by the fall I was waking up stiff almost every day.

Doctor: How long does the morning stiffness last?

Patient: At least an hour, sometimes two. I feel like my hands are stuck. It takes a while before I can make a fist or hold a coffee mug without dropping it.

Doctor: Which joints are affected?

Patient: Mostly the knuckles in my fingers, both hands, my wrists, and my knees. My feet hurt too, especially the balls of my feet when I first get out of bed.

Doctor: Is it on both sides of your body?

Patient: Yes, both hands and both knees. Some days one side is worse, but it’s pretty much both.

Doctor: Any swelling, warmth, or redness over the joints?

Patient: My fingers and wrists look puffy, and my rings don’t fit anymore. They feel warm sometimes, but not really red.

Doctor: How would you describe the pain—aching, sharp, throbbing?

Patient: Mostly aching and throbbing. If I twist or grip something, it can be sharp.

Doctor: Does activity make it better or worse?

Patient: If I sit too long, I get really stiff. Moving around a bit helps loosen things up, but if I overdo it, the pain gets worse later.

Doctor: Any back pain, especially in the lower back or buttocks, that’s worse in the morning?

Patient: My lower back aches sometimes, but it’s not as bad as my hands and knees. It doesn’t wake me up at night.

Doctor: Any rashes, mouth sores, hair loss, or sensitivity to sunlight?

Patient: I get canker sores in my mouth every few months. My hair seems thinner, but I thought that was age. No big rashes, but I do get red on my cheeks if I’m in the sun too long.

Doctor: Any dry eyes or dry mouth?

Patient: My eyes feel gritty sometimes, and I sip water a lot because my mouth feels dry, especially at night.

Doctor: Any fevers, weight loss, or night sweats?

Patient: I’ve had low-grade fevers off and on—99, 100. I’ve lost about 10 pounds in the last six months without trying. I wake up sweaty a couple of nights a week.

Doctor: Any chest pain, shortness of breath, or heart palpitations?

Patient: Sometimes I get a sharp pain when I take a deep breath, on the left side. It comes and goes. I get winded going up stairs, but I thought that was being out of shape.

Doctor: Any numbness or tingling in your hands or feet?

Patient: My fingers tingle sometimes, especially at night. I drop things more often.

Doctor: Any history of blood clots, miscarriages, or strokes?

Patient: No blood clots or strokes. I had two miscarriages in my 20s before I had my two kids.

Doctor: Let’s review your medical history. What other conditions do you have?

Patient: High blood pressure, high cholesterol, and I was told I have “borderline” diabetes. I also have acid reflux. No other big problems.

Doctor: What medications are you taking?

Patient: Lisinopril 10 milligrams once a day, atorvastatin 20 at night, omeprazole 20 once a day, and a multivitamin. For the pain, I take ibuprofen 400 milligrams a few times a day, and sometimes acetaminophen. My primary doctor gave me a short course of prednisone a couple of months ago, 10 milligrams a day for a week, and I felt amazing while I was on it, but the pain came back after I stopped.

Doctor: Any allergies to medications?

Patient: Penicillin gives me a rash. No other drug allergies.

Doctor: What do you do for work?

Patient: I’m an administrative assistant. Lots of typing and filing.

Doctor: Has this affected your ability to work?

Patient: Yes. Typing hurts, and my fingers get stiff. I’ve had to take more breaks. Some days I can’t open jars or file heavy folders.

Doctor: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?

Patient: I quit smoking 10 years ago. I used to smoke about a pack a day. I drink a glass of wine maybe once a week.

Doctor: Any family history of autoimmune diseases, like rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, or thyroid disease?

Patient: My mom had rheumatoid arthritis. Her hands were really deformed by the time she was in her 60s. My sister has hypothyroidism.

Doctor: Since you were referred here, we’ve done some tests. I want to go over them with you. Your primary doctor’s labs showed a positive rheumatoid factor and a positive ANA, so they sent you to us. We repeated and expanded the tests.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: Your labs here show:
- Rheumatoid factor is elevated.
- Anti-CCP antibody is strongly positive.
- ANA is positive at 1:160 with a speckled pattern.
- ESR and CRP, markers of inflammation, are both elevated.
- Your CBC shows mild anemia, hemoglobin 10.8, and a slightly low white count.
- Your kidney and liver tests are normal.
- Your urinalysis is normal—no blood or protein.

Patient: What does that mean?

Doctor: The combination of your symptoms—symmetric small joint pain and swelling, prolonged morning stiffness, fatigue—and the positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP strongly point to rheumatoid arthritis. The positive ANA and some of your other symptoms (mouth sores, dry eyes/mouth, pleuritic chest pain, miscarriages) raise concern for possible overlap with another autoimmune condition, like lupus or Sjögren’s, but right now the picture is most consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.

Patient: So I have what my mom had?

Doctor: You have rheumatoid arthritis, yes, but we’ve caught it earlier than many people in your mother’s generation. We also have better treatments now that can slow or stop joint damage.

Patient: Am I going to end up with twisted hands like hers?

Doctor: Our goal is to prevent that. Early, aggressive treatment with disease-modifying medications can significantly reduce the risk of deformities and disability. The short course of prednisone you took and how much better you felt is another clue that this is inflammatory arthritis.

Patient: What about the positive ANA? Does that mean I have lupus too?

Doctor: ANA can be positive in several autoimmune diseases and even in some healthy people. Your specific lupus markers (like anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) are negative, and your kidneys and blood counts are relatively okay. You have some features that we’ll keep an eye on, but you don’t meet criteria for lupus at this time. We’ll monitor over time.

Patient: How do you treat rheumatoid arthritis?

Doctor: We use medications called DMARDs—disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs—to control the immune system and reduce inflammation. The most common first-line medication is methotrexate. Depending on your response, we may add other medications or biologic agents later.

Patient: Is methotrexate chemotherapy?

Doctor: Methotrexate is used at much higher doses in cancer treatment. For rheumatoid arthritis, we use much lower weekly doses. It’s still a strong medication and requires monitoring, but most people tolerate it well.

Patient: What about the ibuprofen I’ve been taking?

Doctor: NSAIDs like ibuprofen can help with pain, but they don’t stop the disease from progressing and can irritate your stomach and kidneys, especially with your reflux and borderline diabetes. We’ll try to reduce your reliance on NSAIDs as the DMARDs start working. We may use a short course of low-dose prednisone to bridge the time until the DMARD takes effect.

Patient: Will I be on these medicines forever?

Doctor: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic condition. Many people stay on DMARDs long term to keep the disease under control. Sometimes we can reduce doses or simplify the regimen if you’re in sustained remission, but we don’t usually stop treatment completely.

Patient: What about my fatigue? I feel wiped out all the time.

Doctor: The inflammation itself can cause fatigue, and your mild anemia contributes as well. As we control the disease, your energy should improve. We’ll also check your iron, B12, and thyroid if not already done.

Patient: Is there anything I can do besides medicine?

Doctor: Yes:
- Gentle exercise and physical/occupational therapy to maintain joint function.
- Joint protection techniques and ergonomic adjustments at work.
- Weight management and a balanced diet.
- Avoiding smoking, which worsens RA and reduces medication effectiveness.

Patient: Okay. It’s a lot to take in.

Doctor: It is. We’ll go step by step. I’ll outline a treatment plan, and we’ll monitor you closely for side effects and response. We’ll also coordinate with your primary doctor and, if needed, an ophthalmologist for your dry eyes and any medication monitoring.

Patient: Alright. I just don’t want to end up disabled.

Doctor: That’s our goal too: to control the disease, protect your joints, and keep you functioning as well as possible.

Doctor: Good afternoon, Ms. Rivera. I’m Dr. Chang, the endocrinologist. I see you were referred after your recent hospital stay for very high blood sugars and something called DKA. I’d like to go through what happened and how you’re doing now. Is that okay?

Patient: Yes, please. It was really scary. They said I almost went into a coma.

Doctor: Let’s start with how you’re feeling today.

Patient: I’m better than in the hospital, but I’m still tired. My mouth feels dry a lot, and I’m scared of my sugars going crazy again.

Doctor: Tell me about what led up to the hospitalization. What were you noticing in the days or weeks before you went in?

Patient: For about two weeks before, I was really thirsty all the time. I was drinking water constantly and still felt dry. I was peeing a lot, even at night. I lost about 10 pounds without trying. I thought maybe it was stress.

Doctor: Any nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain?

Patient: The last two days before I went to the ER, I felt nauseated and threw up a couple of times. My stomach hurt, kind of a dull ache. I was so tired I could barely get out of bed.

Doctor: Did you check your blood sugar during that time?

Patient: I did. My meter just said “HI” a couple of times. Before that, it was in the 300s and 400s. I didn’t know what to do.

Doctor: What made you decide to go to the hospital?

Patient: I started breathing weird—my husband said I was breathing fast and deep—and I felt confused. He checked my sugar and it just said “HI” again, so he called 911.

Doctor: In the hospital, they told you that you had diabetic ketoacidosis, or DKA. Do you remember what they did for you?

Patient: They put me on an insulin drip and gave me a lot of fluids through the IV. I was in the ICU for a day, then on the regular floor for two more days. They said my blood was “acidic” and my potassium was off.

Doctor: Right. I’ve reviewed your hospital records. When you arrived, your blood sugar was 612, your bicarbonate was 12, your anion gap was 24, and you had positive ketones in your blood and urine. Your pH was 7.21. Your potassium was 5.8 at first, then dropped as they treated you. They diagnosed DKA on top of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes.

Patient: I thought DKA was only for type 1 diabetes.

Doctor: It’s more common in type 1, but it can happen in type 2, especially if insulin is stopped, during infections, or with certain medications. Let’s talk about your diabetes history. When were you diagnosed?

Patient: About 12 years ago, when I was 40. At first they put me on metformin, then added other pills. I started insulin about 3 years ago.

Doctor: What were you taking for your diabetes before this hospitalization?

Patient: Metformin 1000 twice a day, glipizide 10 milligrams in the morning, and insulin glargine 30 units at night. About six months ago, my primary added Jardiance, 10 milligrams in the morning.

Doctor: Were you taking all of those regularly?

Patient: I was pretty good with the pills. The insulin… I missed it maybe once or twice a week. And about a month before I went to the hospital, I stopped the insulin completely for a while.

Doctor: Why did you stop?

Patient: I was trying to lose weight and I read online that insulin makes you gain weight. I thought if I just took the pills and watched what I ate, I’d be okay. I didn’t tell my doctor.

Doctor: Thank you for being honest. Any recent infections, like urinary tract infections, skin infections, or dental issues?

Patient: I had a bad toothache about three weeks before. My dentist said I needed a root canal, but I haven’t had it done yet. The gum was swollen for a while.

Doctor: Any fevers or chills?

Patient: I felt hot and cold, but I didn’t check my temperature.

Doctor: Any other medical conditions?

Patient: High blood pressure, high cholesterol, and I was told my kidneys are “a little weak”—stage 2 kidney disease, I think. I also have fatty liver. No heart attacks or strokes.

Doctor: What medications are you on now, after discharge?

Patient: They changed a lot. Now I’m on metformin 500 twice a day, they stopped the glipizide and Jardiance. I take insulin glargine 24 units at night and insulin lispro 6 units before each meal, plus more if my sugar is high. For blood pressure, I take losartan 50 milligrams once a day and amlodipine 5 milligrams once a day. For cholesterol, atorvastatin 40 milligrams at night. And a baby aspirin.

Doctor: Are you taking the insulin as prescribed now?

Patient: I am. I’m scared not to. I’ve been checking my sugar four times a day like they told me.

Doctor: What have your readings been like since you got home?

Patient: Fasting is around 140 to 180. Before meals it’s 150 to 200. After meals it can go up to 220 or 240. Nothing like before, but still high.

Doctor: Any low sugars? Shakiness, sweating, confusion?

Patient: Once it was 72 before lunch. I felt a little shaky, so I drank some juice. Otherwise, mostly high.

Doctor: How’s your diet been?

Patient: Better than before. I cut out regular soda and juice. I’m trying to eat smaller portions and less rice and tortillas, but it’s hard. I met with the diabetes educator in the hospital, but it was a lot of information.

Doctor: Any problems with your feet? Numbness, tingling, sores?

Patient: My feet tingle sometimes, especially at night, but no sores. I check them every day now like they told me.

Doctor: Any vision changes?

Patient: Things got blurry when my sugar was really high in the hospital, but it’s better now. I haven’t had an eye exam in about a year and a half.

Doctor: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?

Patient: I don’t smoke. I drink a glass of wine maybe once a month.

Doctor: Any family history of diabetes?

Patient: My mom had type 2 diabetes and was on insulin. She had kidney failure and was on dialysis before she died.

Doctor: I’m going to review your vitals and exam now. Your blood pressure today is 138 over 84, heart rate 78, respiratory rate 14, temperature 98.4°F, oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Your weight is 198 pounds; you were 210 at admission and 202 at discharge. Your BMI is 31.

Patient: So I lost weight?

Doctor: Some of that was from being dehydrated in DKA, but you’ve also kept a few pounds off, which is good. On exam, your heart sounds are regular, lungs are clear, abdomen is soft and non-tender. I’ll check your feet now.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: Your feet have intact skin, no ulcers or calluses. Pulses are present. Monofilament testing shows slightly decreased sensation at the tips of your toes, but you can feel it on the balls of your feet and heels. No significant deformities.

Patient: That’s good, right?

Doctor: Yes, but it’s an early sign of nerve involvement, so we want to prevent it from getting worse. Your labs from this morning show your A1c is 11.2%—that reflects the last 3 months, including the time before your hospitalization. Your creatinine is 1.1, eGFR about 70, so your kidneys are stable. Your electrolytes are normal now. Your LDL cholesterol is 126, HDL 40, triglycerides 230. Your urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is 45 mg/g, which is mildly elevated.

Patient: So I really messed things up?

Doctor: You had a serious complication, but you survived it and you’re here now. We can use this as a turning point. DKA tells us your body needs insulin and that stopping it isn’t safe. We need a plan that controls your sugars, fits your life, and that you feel comfortable sticking with.

Patient: I’m scared it’ll happen again.

Doctor: That’s understandable. We’ll:
- Adjust your insulin doses based on your current readings.
- Give you a clear “sick day” plan—what to do if you’re ill, vomiting, or your sugars are very high.
- Decide whether and when to restart certain pills like SGLT2 inhibitors, weighing the benefits and DKA risk.
- Arrange diabetes education and nutrition follow-up.
- Address your dental infection, which may have contributed.

Patient: I still need that root canal.

Doctor: Yes, we’ll coordinate with your dentist and make sure your sugars are reasonably controlled before the procedure. We’ll also schedule an eye exam and keep an eye on your kidneys and nerves.

Patient: Okay. I just don’t want to end up in the ICU again.

Doctor: That’s our goal too. Let’s go through your insulin regimen, your glucose log, and then I’ll outline a detailed plan for home, including when to call us or go to the ER.

Patient: Thank you. I really need a clear plan.

Doctor: We’ll make one together today.
